verses.text: After these things — after Isaac is born, weaned, secured — God tests Abraham. The Hebrew word for tested doesn't mean to trip up; it means to prove, to refine. The test comes not at the beginning of faith but well into it.

Take now thy son, thine only son, whom thou lovest, Isaac — every word lands like a hammer. Then: offer him for a burnt offering upon a mountain I will tell you. The God who gave him is now asking him back.
Abraham rose early in the morning, saddled his donkey, took two young men and Isaac, cut the wood, and went. No protest, no delay. The man who once argued for Sodom does not argue here.

On the third day, Abraham sees the mountain in the distance. Stay here, he tells the servants — the lad and I will go, worship, and come again to you. We will come again. He says it, perhaps already believing what Hebrews later confirms: that God could raise Isaac from the dead.

On the third day, Abraham sees the mountain in the distance. Stay here, he tells the servants — the lad and I will go, worship, and come again to you. We will come again. He says it, perhaps already believing what Hebrews later confirms: that God could raise Isaac from the dead.

Abraham puts the wood on Isaac's shoulders. He himself carries the fire and the knife. They walk on together. The son carries the wood for his own offering — a picture later readers cannot look at without thinking of another Son.

Isaac asks: where is the lamb for a burnt offering? Abraham answers: God will provide Himself a lamb, my son. And they went both together. Abraham's words are not a dodge — they are prophecy he doesn't yet understand.

Isaac asks: where is the lamb for a burnt offering? Abraham answers: God will provide Himself a lamb, my son. And they went both together. Abraham's words are not a dodge — they are prophecy he doesn't yet understand.
They reach the place. Abraham builds the altar, lays the wood, binds Isaac, places him on the wood. He stretches out his hand and takes the knife. There is no mention of Isaac resisting.
They reach the place. Abraham builds the altar, lays the wood, binds Isaac, places him on the wood. He stretches out his hand and takes the knife. There is no mention of Isaac resisting.

Then a voice from heaven: Abraham, Abraham. The name is called twice — the way God calls when He needs us to stop. Lay not thy hand upon the lad. Now I know that thou fearest God, since thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son, from me.

Then a voice from heaven: Abraham, Abraham. The name is called twice — the way God calls when He needs us to stop. Lay not thy hand upon the lad. Now I know that thou fearest God, since thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son, from me.
Abraham lifts his eyes and sees a ram caught in a thicket by its horns. Provision was already there, waiting. He offers the ram in place of Isaac. The lamb God promised has come.

Abraham names the place Jehovah-jireh — the LORD will provide. From that day on, mount Moriah carried the saying: in the mount of the LORD it shall be seen. Centuries later, the temple of Solomon would be built on this same mountain.

The angel calls a second time, with the strongest oath in Scripture: by myself have I sworn. Because you have done this and not withheld your son, I will bless you, multiply your offspring like stars and sand, and in your seed all nations of the earth will be blessed. The promise is sealed by a sacrifice — and points ahead to one even greater.

The angel calls a second time, with the strongest oath in Scripture: by myself have I sworn. Because you have done this and not withheld your son, I will bless you, multiply your offspring like stars and sand, and in your seed all nations of the earth will be blessed. The promise is sealed by a sacrifice — and points ahead to one even greater.
Abraham returns to the servants — without explanation — and they all go back to Beersheba together. The walk down the mountain is silent in the text. Some things are too sacred to retell.

Genesis closes the chapter with quiet family news from far away. Abraham's brother Nahor has eight sons by his wife Milcah — including Bethuel, who fathers Rebekah. The next wife for the next chapter of the promise is already being prepared.
